reason: The item cleanly tests boundary-aware interpretation of thermal runaway metrics (onset vs peak hazard measures) and avoids requiring external knowledge beyond the provided comparisons, while still allowing limited mechanistic inference from gas composition. Decoys are largely single-fault and engineering-plausible, though a couple lean toward obviously overbroad language ("all confinement configurations", "sufficient indicator"), slightly reducing discriminative difficulty among experts. Overall, it provides strong diagnostic value on extrapolation control and metric/composition reasoning without hidden assumptions dominating the correct set.

The item cleanly targets boundary-aware judgment about whether SOC reduction yields proportional hazard mitigation across multiple metrics, with a clear regime-change cue (secondary ignition) that forces experts to separate combustion-driven HRR from mechanical venting/pressure hazards. Quantitative anchors make overconfident linear-scaling answers fail while still allowing a coherent engineering implication about multi-dimensional hazard controls. Some residual ambiguity remains because “proportionally” is not formally defined (ratio tolerance not specified), but the magnitude differences are strong enough to preserve discrimination.
